name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayah

```arabic
أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ لَيْلَةَ ٱلصِّيَامِ ٱلرَّفَثُ إِلَىٰ نِسَآئِكُمْ ۚ هُنَّ لِبَاسٌ لَّكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لِبَاسٌ لَّهُنَّ ۗ عَلِمَ ٱللَّهُ أَنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ تَخْتَانُونَ أَنفُسَكُمْ فَتَابَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَعَفَا عَنكُمْ ۖ فَٱلْـَٔـٰنَ بَـٰشِرُوهُنَّ وَٱبْتَغُوا۟ مَا كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ۚ وَكُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَتَبَيَّنَ لَكُمُ ٱلْخَيْطُ ٱلْأَبْيَضُ مِنَ ٱلْخَيْطِ ٱلْأَسْوَدِ مِنَ ٱلْفَجْرِ ۖ ثُمَّ أَتِمُّوا۟ ٱلصِّيَامَ إِلَى ٱلَّيْلِ ۚ وَلَا تُبَـٰشِرُوهُنَّ وَأَنتُمْ عَـٰكِفُونَ فِى ٱلْمَسَـٰجِدِ ۗ تِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ فَلَا تَقْرَبُوهَا ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ (١٨٧)
```

```english
You are permitted on the night of the fast to go into your wives: they are a garment for you, and you are a garment for them. Allah knew that you would betray yourselves, so He pardoned you and excused you. So now consort with them and seek what Allah has ordained for you, and eat and drink until the white streak becomes manifest to you from the dark streak at the crack of dawn. Then complete the fast until nightfall, and do not consort with them while you dwell in confinement in the mosques. These are Allah’s bounds, so do not approach them. Thus does Allah clarify His signs for mankind so that they may be Godwary. (187)
```

## (Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: **أُحل لكم ليلة الصيام الرفث إلى نسائكم** (Permitted to you on the night of the fast is goings-in to your wives): Permissibility (الإحلال) carries the meaning of authorization. Its root is from "untying" (الحل), which is the opposite of "binding" (العقد). "Goings-in" (الرفث) refers to the explicit mention of that which is usually referred to by allusion (التكنية) due to the perceived indecency of its mention—specifically those terms associated with sexual intimacy with women. Here, it is used as an allusion for the act of sexual intercourse (الجماع), which is part of the refined literary style (أدب) of the Noble Quran. Similarly, other terms used in the Quran, such as "consorting" (المباشرة), "entering" (الدخول), "touching" (المس), "feeling" (اللمس), "coming unto" (الإتيان), and "drawing near" (القرب), are all terms employed by way of allusion. Likewise, terms like "treading" (الوطء) and "intercourse" (الجماع) used outside the Quran are allusive terms, even if frequent usage has moved some of them from the boundary of allusion to explicit statement. This is similar to how the terms "private parts" (الفرج) and "excrement" (الغائط), in their modern known meanings, belong to this category. The use of the preposition "to" (إلى) with "goings-in" (الرفث) is because it incorporates the meaning of "reaching" or "attaining" (الإفضاء), as has been suggested.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هن لباس لكم وأنتم لباس لهن** (They are a garment for you, and you are a garment for them): The apparent meaning of "garment" (اللباس) is its well-known sense: that which a person uses to cover their body. The two sentences are a form of metaphor (استعارة), for each spouse prevents the other from following lewdness and spreading it among the members of the species. Thus, each of them is a garment for the other, concealing their "shameful parts" (السوأة) and covering their "nakedness" (العورة).

This is a subtle metaphor, and its subtlety increases when joined with His saying: **أُحل لكم ليلة الصيام الرفث إلى نسائكم** (Permitted to you on the night of the fast is goings-in to your wives). For a person conceals their nakedness from others with a garment, but as for the garment itself, there is no concealment from it. Similarly, each spouse protects the other from "goings-in" (الرفث) toward anyone else, but as for "goings-in" toward the spouse, it is not prohibited, because the spouse is the garment attached to one's self and in direct contact with it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **علم الله أنكم كنتم تختانون أنفسكم فتاب عليكم وعفا عنكم** (Allah knew that you used to betray yourselves, so He turned to you [clemently] and excused you): "Betrayal" (الاختيان) and "treachery" (الخيانة) carry the same meaning, and it contains the sense of "deficiency" (النقص), as has been said. In His saying: **أنكم تختانون** (that you used to betray), there is an indication of continuity (الاستمرار). Thus, the verse indicates that this betrayal was a recurring and continuous practice among the Muslims since the ruling of fasting was first legislated. They were secretly disobeying Allah the Exalted by betraying themselves. Had this betrayal not been a sin (معصية), He would not have sent down "repentance" (التوبة) and "pardon" (العفو). Although these two terms are not explicit in indicating a prior sin, they—especially when joined together—clearly suggest it.

Based on this, the verse indicates that the ruling of fasting before the revelation of this verse was the prohibition of sexual intercourse during the night of the fast. With its revelation, the verse legislated permissibility and abrogated (نسخت) the prohibition, as mentioned by a group of exegetes. This is sensed or indicated by His saying: **أُحل لكم** (Permitted to you), His saying: **كنتم تختانون** (you used to betray), His saying: **فتاب عليكم وعفا عنكم** (so He turned to you and excused you), and His saying: **فالآن باشروهن** (So now consort with them). For if there had been no prior prohibition, the appropriate phrasing would have been: "There is no blame upon you if you consort with them," or something conveying that meaning, which is evident.

It is sometimes said that the verse is not abrogating (ناسخة) because there is no prohibitory ruling in the verses of fasting regarding sexual intercourse or eating and drinking. Rather, as suggested by some narrations (روايات) transmitted through the paths of the *Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama'ah*, when the ruling of the obligation of fasting was revealed and the Muslims heard His saying: **كتب عليكم الصيام كما كتب على الذين من قبلكم** (Fasting has been prescribed for you as it was prescribed for those before you), they understood from it an equality in rulings in all aspects. The Christians, as it is said, used to marry, eat, and drink in the early part of the night, then abstain after that. The Muslims adopted this, but it became difficult for them. The young men among them would not refrain from marriage secretly, while viewing it as a sin and a betrayal of themselves. The elderly were perhaps exhausted by refraining from eating and drinking after sleeping; sometimes sleep would overcome one of them, and he would believe eating and drinking had become prohibited for him. Thus, the verse was revealed to clarify that marriage, eating, and drinking are not prohibited for them during the night in the month of Ramadan. It thereby became clear that the intent of the verse's comparison in His saying: **كما كتب على الذين من قبلكم** (as it was prescribed for those before you) was a comparison in the essence of the obligation of fasting, not in its specific details. As for His saying: **أُحل لكم** (Permitted to you), it does not indicate a prior prohibitory ruling, but merely the realization of permissibility, as in His saying: **أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ صَيْدُ الْبَحْرِ** (Permitted to you is the game of the sea) [5:96], for it is known that sea game was not prohibited for those in the state of *ihram* before the revelation of that verse. Similarly, His saying: **عَلِمَ اللَّهُ أَنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ تَخْتَانُونَ أَنفُسَكُمْ** (Allah knew that you used to betray yourselves) only means that they were betraying according to their own assumption and belief that it was betrayal and sin. Therefore, He said: **تختانون أنفسكم** (betray yourselves) and did not say: "betray Allah," as He said: **لَا تَخُونُوا اللَّهَ وَالرَّسُولَ وَتَخُونُوا أَمَانَاتِكُمْ** (do not betray Allah and the Apostle, and do not betray your trusts) [8:27]. Furthermore, it is possible that "betrayal" (الاختيان) means "deficiency" (النقص), meaning: Allah knew that you were depriving yourselves of their share of desires such as marriage and other things. Likewise, His saying: **فتاب عليكم وعفا عنكم** (so He turned to you and excused you) is not explicit in marriage being a prohibited sin.

However, there is a response to this: what you have seen is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse. For His saying: **أُحل لكم** (Permitted to you), His saying: **كنتم تختانون أنفسكم** (you used to betray yourselves), and His saying: **فتاب عليكم وعفا عنكم** (so He turned to you and excused you), even if not explicit in abrogation (النسخ), possess a complete appearance of indicating it. This is in addition to His saying: **فالآن باشروهن** (So now consort with them) etc., for if there had been nothing but a continuous permissibility before and after the revelation of the verse, there would be no apparent reason for this expression. As for the previous verses of fasting not containing the prohibitory ruling, this does not contradict the verse being abrogating. For those verses did not clarify the other rulings of fasting either, such as the prohibition of marriage, eating, and drinking during the day of the fast. It is known that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had clarified this to the Muslims before the revelation of this verse. Perhaps he had clarified this ruling among the rulings he explained, and the verse abrogates what the Messenger clarified, even if His (Allah's) speech did not contain it.

**If you say:** His saying: **هن لباس لكم وأنتم لباس لهن** (They are a garment for you, and you are a garment for them) indicates the reason for legislating the permissibility of "goings-in" (الرفث). Therefore, it must not encompass both the abrogating and the abrogated, for it would be unseemly to justify abrogation with something that applies to both the abrogating and the abrogated together—even if we say that these justifications occurring in the context of rulings are wisdoms (حكم) and interests (مصالح) rather than strict causes (علل), and it is not required for a "wisdom" to be all-inclusive and exclusive like "causes." If "goings-in" were prohibited before the revelation of the verse and then abrogated by the permitting verse, it would not be sound to justify the abrogation of the prohibition by saying that men are garments for women and they are garments for them.

**I say: Firstly,** this is refuted by the restriction of His saying: **أُحل لكم** (Permitted to you) with His saying: **ليلة الصيام** (on the night of the fast), despite the fact that the ruling of "garment" (اللباس) applies during the day just as it does at night, yet it is prohibited during the day. **Secondly,** the conditions taken in the verse from His saying: **ليلة الصيام** (on the night of the fast), His saying: **هن لباس لكم** (they are a garment for you), and His saying: **أنكم كنتم تختانون أنفسكم** (that you used to betray yourselves), indicate three ordered causes (علل) upon which the abrogated and abrogating rulings are based. The fact that one spouse is a garment for the other necessitates that "goings-in" (الرفث) between them be permitted absolutely. Then, the ruling of fasting indicated by His saying: **ليلة الصيام** (on the night of the fast)—and fasting (الصيام) is the refraining and abstaining from the desires of the soul such as eating, drinking, and marriage—necessitates restricting the permissibility of "goings-in" and diverting it away from the occasion of fasting. Then, the difficulty for them to restrain the soul from marriage for an entire month and their falling into continuous sin and ongoing betrayal necessitates a certain ease for them by granting a license for that at night. By this, the absolute nature of the ruling of "garment" (اللباس) restricted by fasting returns to some of its absolute application—namely, that it be acted upon at night and not during the day. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: the absolute nature of the ruling of "garment" which We restricted by fasting during the night and day and prohibited for you, We have now made permissible for you because We knew you were betraying yourselves therein, and We intended to lighten your burden out of kindness and mercy. We have restored the absolute nature of that ruling to it during the night of the fast and limited the ruling of fasting to the day; so complete the fast until the night.

In summary: His saying: **هن لباس لكم وأنتم لباس لهن** (They are a garment for you, and you are a garment for them), even if it is a cause (عللة) or wisdom (حكمة) for the permissibility of the essence of "goings-in" (الرفث), the purpose in the verse is not directed toward it alone. Rather, the purpose is to clarify the wisdom of the permissibility of "goings-in" on the night of the fast, which is the totality of His saying: **هن لباس لكم** (They are a garment for you) until His saying: **وعفا عنكم** (and excused you). This wisdom is limited to the abrogating ruling and certainly does not encompass the abrogated one.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فالآن باشروهن وابتغوا ما كتب الله لكم** (So now consort with them and seek what Allah has ordained for you): This is a command occurring after a prohibition, thus indicating permissibility. It was preceded by His saying at the beginning of the verse: **أُحل لكم** (Permitted to you). The meaning is: from now on, consorting with them is permissible for you. "Seeking" (الابتغاء) is to search for or desire. What is meant by "seeking what Allah has ordained" is the seeking of offspring, which Allah the Glorified has ordained for the human species through the path of consorting. He fashioned their primordial nature (فطرهم) to seek it through the desire for marriage and consorting that He placed within them. He subjected them through this to this act; thus, they seek through it what Allah has ordained for them, even if they apparently only intend to satisfy desire and attain pleasure. This is just as He ordained for them the preservation of life and growth through eating and drinking, which is the primordial objective, even if they only intend by eating and drinking to obtain the pleasure of taste, satiety, and quenching thirst; for it is indeed a divine subjection (تسخير).

As for what has been said—that what is meant by "what Allah has ordained for them" is the permissibility and the license (الرخصة), for Allah loves that His licenses be taken just as He loves that His firm obligations (عزائمه) be taken—this is far-fetched because "ordaining" (الكتابة) in His speech is not customarily used in the context of permissibility and license.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وكلوا واشربوا حتى يتبين لكم الخيط الأبيض من الخيط الأسود من الفجر** (and eat and drink until the white streak becomes manifest to you from the black streak at dawn): There are two dawns (الفجر). The first dawn is called the "false dawn" (الكاذب) because it vanishes after a short stay, and it is called the "wolf's tail" (ذنب السرحان) because of its resemblance to a wolf's tail when it is raised. It is a vertical column of light that appears at the end of the night in the eastern horizon when the sun's distance from the horizon reaches eighteen degrees below it. Then it vanishes by becoming horizontal, becoming a broad, elongated streak on the horizon like a white thread stretched over it; this is the second dawn. It is called the "true dawn" (الفجر الصادق) because of its truthfulness in what it reports and announces regarding the arrival of the day and its connection to the rising of the sun.

From this, it is known that what is meant by the "white streak" (الخيط الأبيض) is the true dawn, and that the word "from" (من) is explanatory (بيانية). His saying: **حتى يتبين لكم الخيط الأسود** (until the black streak becomes manifest to you) is a form of metaphor (استعارة) by likening the whiteness spreading across the horizon from the dawn, adjacent to the darkness of the night extending horizontally with it, to a white thread that becomes distinct from a black thread.

From this, it is also known that what is meant is the delimitation by the first moment of the rising of the true dawn, for the rising of the light of the day's whiteness nullifies the two "threads"; thus, there is no longer a white thread nor a black thread.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثم أتموا الصيام إلى الليل** (then complete the fast until the night): Since the delimitation by the dawn indicated the obligation of fasting until the night after it becomes manifest, He dispensed with mentioning its beginning, preferring brevity. Rather, He addressed its delimitation by "completing" (إتمام) it until the night. In His saying: **أتموا** (complete), there is an indication that it is a single, simple entity—a single, complete act of worship—rather than being composed of numerous matters, each of which is an independent act of worship. This is the difference between "completion" (التمام) and "perfection" (الكمال), where the former is the end of the existence of that which is not composed of parts having independent effects, and the latter is the end of the existence of that which each of its parts has an independent effect on its own. The Exalted said: **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي** (Today I have perfected for you your religion, and completed My blessing upon you) [5:3]. For the religion (الدين) is the sum of prayer, fasting, pilgrimage, and others, each of which has an effect it is independent in, unlike the "blessing" (النعمة), as the explanation of this will come, Allah willing, in the discussion of that verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ولا تباشروهن وأنتم عاكفون في المساجد** (and do not consort with them while you are in retreat in the mosques): "Staying" (العكوف) and "retreat" (الاعتكاف) mean remaining or adhering to something. Retreat in a place is to reside in it, adhering to it.

Retreat (الاعتكاف) is a specific act of worship, among whose rulings are remaining in the mosque and not leaving it except for an excuse, and fasting during it. Therefore, it was possible to imagine the permissibility of consorting with women during the nights of retreat in the mosque due to the legislation of the permissibility of "goings-in" (الرفث) on the night of the fast. Thus, He repelled this potential misunderstanding with His saying: **ولا تباشروهن وأنتم عاكفون في المساجد** (and do not consort with them while you are in retreat in the mosques).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **تلك حدود الله فلا تقربوها** (These are the bounds of Allah, so do not approach them): The root of "bound" (الحد) is "prevention" (المنع), and to this return all its usages and derivations, such as the "edge" (حد) of a sword, the "legal punishment" (حد) for lewdness, the "boundary" (حد) of a house, and "iron" (الحديد), among others. The prohibition against "approaching" (القرب) the bounds is an allusion (كناية) for not committing them or transgressing toward them. That is: do not commit these sins—which are eating, drinking, and consorting—or do not transgress these divine rulings and prohibitions which He has clarified for you, which are the rulings of fasting, by wasting them and abandoning God-consciousness (التقوى) regarding them.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: **كان الاكل والنكاح محرمين في شهر رمضان بالليل بعد النوم يعني كل من صلى العشاء ونام ولم يفطر ثم انتبه حرم عليه الافطار ، وكان النكاح حراماً في الليل والنهار في شهر رمضان ، وكان رجل من أصحاب رسول الله يقال له خوّات بن جبير الانصاري اخو عبد الله بن جبير الذي كان رسول الله وكله بفم الشِعب يوم أُحد في خمسين من الرماة ففارقه أصحابه وبقي في اثنى عشر رجلاً فقتل على باب الشعب ، وكان اخوه هذا خوّات بن جبير شيخاً كبيراً ضعيفاً ، وكان صائماً مع رسول الله في الخندق ، فجاء إلى أهله حين أمسى فقال عندكم طعام ؟ فقالوا : لا تنم حتى نصنع لك طعاماً فأبطأت عليه اهله بالطعام فنام قبل ان يفطر ، فلما انتبه قال لأهله ، قد حرم على الاكل في هذه الليلة فلما اصبح حضر حفر الخندق فاغمى عليه فرآه رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم فرقّ له وكان قوم من الشبان ينكحون بالليل سراً في شهر رمضان فأنزل الله : أُحل لكم ليلة الصيام الرفث إِلى نسائكم الآية ، فأحل الله تبارك وتعالى النكاح بالليل من شهر رمضان ، والاكل بعد النوم إِلى طلوع الفجر لقوله : حتى يتبين لكم الخيط الابيض من الخيط الاسود من الفجر ، قال : هو بياض النهار من سواد الليل .** — "Eating and marriage were prohibited in the month of Ramadan at night after sleeping. That is, whoever prayed Isha and slept without breaking his fast, then woke up, it was prohibited for him to break his fast. Marriage was prohibited during both the night and the day in the month of Ramadan. There was a man from the companions of the Messenger of Allah called Khawwat ibn Jubayr al-Ansari, the brother of Abdullah ibn Jubayr, whom the Messenger of Allah had entrusted with the mouth of the mountain pass on the day of Uhud among fifty archers; his companions left him and he remained with twelve men and was killed at the gate of the pass. This brother of his, Khawwat ibn Jubayr, was an old and weak man. He was fasting with the Messenger of Allah at the Trench (al-Khandaq). When evening came, he went to his family and said, 'Do you have food?' They said, 'Do not sleep until we prepare food for you.' His family was slow in bringing the food, and he fell asleep before breaking his fast. When he woke up, he said to his family, 'Eating has become prohibited for me this night.' When morning came, he attended the digging of the trench and fainted. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw him and felt pity for him. There were also some young men who were marrying [having intercourse] secretly at night in the month of Ramadan. So Allah revealed: 'Permitted to you on the night of the fast is goings-in to your wives...' the verse. Thus, Allah, Blessed and Exalted, permitted marriage during the night of the month of Ramadan, and eating after sleep until the rising of the dawn, due to His saying: 'until the white streak becomes manifest to you from the black streak at dawn.' He said: 'It is the whiteness of the day from the blackness of the night.'"

**I say:** The phrase "That is..." until "...There was a man" is from the speech of the narrator. This meaning is narrated in other reports transmitted by al-Kulayni, al-Ayyashi, and others. In all of them, the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) for His saying: **وكلوا واشربوا** (and eat and drink) etc., was the story of Khawwat ibn Jubayr al-Ansari, and the occasion of revelation for His saying: **أُحل لكم** (Permitted to you) etc., was what the young men among the Muslims were doing.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, narrated from several scholars of exegesis and tradition from al-Bara' ibn 'Azib, who said: **كان أصحاب النبي صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم إِذا كان الرجل صائماً فحضر الافطار فنام قبل أن يفطر لم يأكل ليلته ولا يومه حتى يمسي وإن قيس بن صرمة الانصاري كان صائماً فكان يومه ذاك يعمل في ارضه فلما حضر الافطار اتى امرأته فقال : هل عندك طعام ؟ قالت : لا ولكن انطلق فأطلب لك فغلبته عينه فنام وجائت امرأته فلما رأته نائماً قالت : خيبة لك ، أنمت ؟ فلما انتصف النهار غشي عليه فذكر ذلك للنبي صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم فنزلت هذه الآية أُحل لكم ليلة الصيام الرفث ، إِلى قوله : من الفجر ففرحوا بها فرحاً شديداً .** — "The companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to be such that if a man was fasting and the time for breaking the fast arrived, but he slept before breaking it, he would not eat that night nor the following day until evening. Qays ibn Sirmah al-Ansari was fasting and had been working in his land that day. When the time for breaking the fast arrived, he came to his wife and said, 'Do you have food?' She said, 'No, but I will go and seek some for you.' His eyes were overcome and he fell asleep. His wife came, and when she saw him sleeping, she said, 'Woe to you, did you sleep?' When midday came, he fainted. This was mentioned to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and this verse was revealed: 'Permitted to you on the night of the fast is goings-in...' until His saying: '...at dawn.' They rejoiced at it with a great joy."

**I say:** The story is narrated through other paths; in some, the name is Abu Qubays ibn Sirmah, and in others, Sirmah ibn Malik al-Ansari, with variations in the details of the story.

Also in *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn Abbas: **ان المسلمين كانوا في شهر رمضان إذا صلوا العشاء حرم عليهم النساء والطعام إلى مثلها من القابلة ، ثم إِن ناساً من المسلمين أصابوا الطعام والنساء في رمضان بعد العشاء ، منهم عمر بن الخطاب فشكوا ذلك الى رسول الله فأنزل الله أُحل لكم ليلة الصيام ، الى قوله : فالآن باشروهن يعني انكحوهن .** — "The Muslims in the month of Ramadan, when they prayed Isha, women and food became prohibited for them until the same time the following day. Then, some people among the Muslims took food and women in Ramadan after Isha, among them Umar ibn al-Khattab. They complained of that to the Messenger of Allah, so Allah revealed: 'Permitted to you on the night of the fast...' until His saying: '...So now consort with them,' meaning: have intercourse with them."

**I say:** The narrations from their paths regarding this meaning are numerous, and in most of them, the name of Umar is mentioned. They are united in that the ruling of marriage at night was like the ruling of eating and drinking: they were all permitted before sleep and prohibited after it. However, the apparent sense of the first narration we cited is that marriage was prohibited in the month of Ramadan during both the night and the day, unlike eating and drinking, which were permitted in the early part of the night before sleep and prohibited after it. The context of the verse supports this, for if marriage had been like eating and drinking—permitted before sleep and prohibited after it—it would have been necessary for the wording to be restricted by a limit (الغاية), as was done with His saying: **كلوا واشربوا حتى يتبين لكم الخيط الأبيض** (eat and drink until the white streak becomes manifest) etc. Yet He said: **أُحل لكم ليلة الصيام الرفث** (Permitted to you on the night of the fast is goings-in), and did not provide a restriction indicating a limit. Likewise, what is contained in some narrations—that the betrayal was not exclusive to marriage but that they were also betraying in eating and drinking—does not agree with what is suggested by the context of the verse in placing His saying: **علم الله أنكم كنتم تختانون أنفسكم** (Allah knew that you used to betray yourselves) etc., before His saying: **وكلوا واشربوا** (and eat and drink).

Also in *al-Durr al-Manthur*: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: **الفجر فجران فأما الذي كأنه ذنب السرحان فإنه لا يحل شيئاً ولا يحرمه ، واما المستطيل الذي يأخذ الافق فإنه يحل الصلوة ويحرّم الطعام .** — "The dawn is of two types: as for that which is like the wolf's tail, it neither permits anything nor prohibits it. As for the elongated one that takes the horizon, it permits the prayer and prohibits food."

**I say:** The narrations regarding this meaning are widely transmitted (مستفيضة) through both the general (*Sunni*) and specific (*Shi'i*) paths, as are the narrations regarding retreat (الاعتكاف) and the prohibition of sexual intercourse during it.
